# Pipes, Redirection, and the Danger Chapter

Chapter 6 gave the project a place for everything: raw data
here, scripts there, output in its own folder. This chapter is
about the plumbing that moves data between those places. Every
command you run reads from somewhere and writes to somewhere,
and the shell lets you re-point both ends: send a command's
output to a file, feed a file into a command, or chain
commands so one's output becomes the next one's input. That
small set of moves, redirection and pipes, is most of what
makes the shell feel like a workshop instead of a list of
isolated tools.

It is also where the shell first turns dangerous. Chapter 2
drew the line between what the shell does and what Git does:
the shell runs commands, Git versions files. The companion Git
book can recover almost anything you have committed. The shell
can destroy, in one keystroke and with no confirmation, things
Git never saw: an uncommitted result, a raw data file you were
told never to commit, a whole directory. There is no reflog
for `rm -rf` and no undo for `>`. So this chapter teaches the
plumbing and the danger together, because in the shell they
are the same lever. The quoting and null-safety rules near the
end are not advanced polish; they are the seatbelt, and they
belong at the beginner tier with everything else.

A note on where the output comes from. The redirection, pipe,
quoting, glob, and `rm` demonstrations are run in this book's
Linux sandbox (Ubuntu 22.04, GNU bash 5.1, GNU coreutils 8.32,
GNU findutils 4.8; the `ch07-*` transcript files). Every
destructive demonstration is run in a throwaway `/tmp`
directory, never in a real project, for reasons that become
obvious by the end. The macOS and BSD divergences, where zsh
and BSD tools behave differently from the Linux baseline, are
captured separately on the author's Mac
(`transcripts/ch07-divergence-mac.txt`, produced by
`capture-ch07-mac.sh`) and called out where they diverge. The one
command this chapter refuses to run anywhere, a blind
`curl | sh`, is shown but never executed, and labeled as such.

## Three streams: stdin, stdout, stderr

Chapter 2 introduced the three standard streams in the
abstract. Here is where they earn their keep. Every command
starts life with three channels already open: **standard
input** (stdin, where it reads from), **standard output**
(stdout, where it writes results), and **standard error**
(stderr, where it writes diagnostics). They carry the numbers
0, 1, and 2. The reason they are separate, rather than one
combined stream, is the whole point: results and complaints
can be sent to different places.

You can see the split with a command that produces both at
once. Asking `ls` to list one directory that exists and one
that does not sends the listing to stdout and the error to
stderr:

```bash
$ ls data/raw nope_dir
ls: cannot access 'nope_dir': No such file or directory
data/raw:
factors.csv
firm_panel.csv
```

On a plain terminal both streams land on the screen, which is
why they look interleaved. They are not the same channel,
though, and you can prove it by silencing one at a time.
Sending stderr to the discard file `/dev/null` leaves the
results untouched:

```bash
$ ls data/raw nope_dir 2>/dev/null
data/raw:
factors.csv
firm_panel.csv
```

Silencing stdout instead leaves only the error behind:

```bash
$ ls data/raw nope_dir 1>/dev/null
ls: cannot access 'nope_dir': No such file or directory
```

The `2>` and `1>` are the next section's subject. The point
for now is that "the output" of a command is really two
streams, and keeping them apart is what lets you save a clean
result while still seeing the warnings, or log the warnings
while letting the result flow on down a pipe.

## Redirecting output to files: `>` and `>>`

The single greater-than, `>`, sends a command's stdout to a
file instead of the screen. If the file does not exist it is
created; if it does exist it is **truncated to empty first**,
then written. The double form, `>>`, appends to the file
instead, creating it only if needed. Counting the rows of the
two raw inputs and collecting both counts in one file shows
the pair at work:

```bash
$ wc -l data/raw/firm_panel.csv >  counts.txt
$ wc -l data/raw/factors.csv    >> counts.txt
$ cat counts.txt
54001 data/raw/firm_panel.csv
181 data/raw/factors.csv
```

The first line creates `counts.txt` and writes the panel's
line count; the second appends the factor file's count below
it. Swap the `>>` for a `>` on the second line and you would
be left with only the factor count, because the second `>`
would empty the file before writing. That is not a
hypothetical:

```bash
$ echo 'first version'  > note.txt
$ echo 'second version' > note.txt
$ cat note.txt
second version
```

The first line is gone. No prompt, no warning, no copy in a
trash folder. The file held `first version` for a fraction of
a second and now it does not.

::: {.callout-caution .danger title="DANGER"}
`>` overwrites an existing file silently and irreversibly.
There is no confirmation and no undo. The classic way to lose
work is to reach for `>` when you meant `>>`, or to redirect
into a file that is also feeding the command (`sort data.csv >
data.csv` truncates `data.csv` to empty before `sort` ever
reads it, destroying the input). bash can refuse to clobber:
set the `noclobber` option and `>` will decline to overwrite
an existing file, with `>|` as the explicit override for the
times you mean it:

```bash
$ set -o noclobber
$ echo 'third version' > note.txt
bash: note.txt: cannot overwrite existing file
$ echo $?
1
$ echo 'forced' >| note.txt
$ cat note.txt
forced
```

Turning on `noclobber` (many people put `set -o noclobber` in
their shell startup file, Chapter 18) converts a silent
overwrite into a loud refusal. It does not protect against
`>|`, against `rm`, or against a script that runs with it
unset, so treat it as one guard rail, not the guard rail.
:::

Redirection has a quieter, constructive use that matters for
reproducibility: it captures what happened. Merging a
command's stdout and stderr into one log file (the `2>&1` is
the next section; the `{ ...; }` that groups the two commands
so one `> run.log` covers both is explained in Chapter 11's
scripting primer) gives you a record you can commit alongside
results, so "what did that run actually print" has an answer
months later:

```bash
$ { echo "run: distinct-firm count"; \
    cut -d, -f1 data/raw/firm_panel.csv \
      | tail -n +2 | sort -u | wc -l; } > run.log 2>&1
$ cat run.log
run: distinct-firm count
300
```

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
A result you cannot trace back to the command that produced it
is a result you have to take on faith. Redirecting a run into a
dated log file, or `make`-ing the report from a graph as in
Chapter 12, is how a shell session leaves a paper trail instead
of a screenful of text that scrolls away. When you want the log
*and* the output on screen at the same time, `tee` (Chapter 11)
splits the stream in two; here a plain `>` is enough. The
discipline is the same one the Git book argues for results:
prefer the artifact you can regenerate and inspect over the one
you remember running.
:::

## Separating errors from results: `2>` and `2>&1` (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

Because stdout and stderr are numbered channels, you redirect
each by its number. `2>` points stderr at a file while stdout
goes wherever it would normally go. Sending the errors to a log
keeps them out of the results without throwing them away:

```bash
$ ls data/raw nope_dir 2> errors.log
data/raw:
factors.csv
firm_panel.csv
$ cat errors.log
ls: cannot access 'nope_dir': No such file or directory
```

To put **both** streams in one file, redirect stdout to the
file and then tie stderr to "wherever stdout now points" with
`2>&1`. The portable spelling puts the file first and the tie
second:

```bash
$ ls data/raw nope_dir > both.log 2>&1
$ cat both.log
ls: cannot access 'nope_dir': No such file or directory
data/raw:
factors.csv
firm_panel.csv
```

The order is not decoration. `2>&1` means "make stderr go where
stdout goes *right now*," so it has to come after the file
redirection. Reverse them and stderr is tied to the screen
(stdout's original destination) before stdout is moved to the
file, so the error misses the file entirely:

```bash
$ ls data/raw nope_dir 2>&1 > only_stdout.log
ls: cannot access 'nope_dir': No such file or directory
$ cat only_stdout.log
data/raw:
factors.csv
firm_panel.csv
```

The error printed to the terminal and `only_stdout.log` caught
just the listing. This trips up nearly everyone once.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
bash and zsh both accept the shorthand `&> file` for "redirect
stdout and stderr to file," and it is convenient interactively.
It is **not portable**: it is undefined in POSIX `sh` [-@posix2018], so a
script that runs under `/bin/sh` (dash on Debian and Ubuntu)
can fail or misbehave with it. The portable form that works in
every Bourne-family shell is `> file 2>&1`, in that order. This
book writes the portable form in scripts and saves `&>` for the
interactive prompt. The redirection-order rule above is the
same on macOS (zsh 5.9, and the system bash 3.2.57) as on
Linux, confirmed in `transcripts/ch07-divergence-mac.txt`.
:::

## Pipes: composing small commands

Redirection connects a command to a file. A **pipe**, the `|`
character, connects a command's stdout directly to the next
command's stdin, with no file in between. This is the idea the
whole Unix toolset is built around: instead of one program that
does everything, many small programs that each do one thing,
strung together so the output of one is the input of the next.

Counting the distinct firms in the panel is a four-stage pipe,
and it answers a real question about the running example:

```bash
$ cut -d, -f1 data/raw/firm_panel.csv | tail -n +2 \
    | sort -u | wc -l
300
```

Read left to right, that is the pipeline in English: take the
first comma-separated column (`cut -d, -f1`, where `-d,` sets
the field delimiter to a comma and `-f1` keeps field 1), drop
the header row (`tail -n +2`), reduce to unique sorted values
(`sort -u`), and count the lines that remain (`wc -l`). The
answer, 300, is the firm count the generator locked in. One
quirk of `tail` earns a note, because it trips people who read
`tail` as only ever counting from the end: `tail -n +2` means
*from* line 2 onward (everything after the header), whereas
`tail -n 2`, like `head -n 2`, means a *count* of 2 lines. The
leading `+` flips the number from how many lines to which line
to start at, and there is no portable `head` mirror for it
(GNU `head -n -K` exists but means all *but* the last K
lines, a different and non-portable job). The
companion month count is a two-stage pipe on the factor file:

```bash
$ tail -n +2 data/raw/factors.csv | wc -l
180
```

The way to build a pipe is the way to read it: one stage at a
time. Run `cut` alone and look; add `tail` and look again. Each
stage sees only the text the previous one emitted, so you can
inspect the stream at any point:

```bash
$ cut -d, -f1 data/raw/firm_panel.csv | head -4
firm
F0000
F0000
F0000
```

The header `firm` is still there, which is exactly why the full
pipe needs the `tail -n +2` to drop it before counting. Pipes
reward this build-and-check habit: a long pipeline that
misbehaves is almost always one stage that is not emitting what
you assumed, and you find it by stopping the pipe one stage
earlier.

## Pipes and exit status: `pipefail` (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

A pipe hides a trap that matters the moment you put one in a
script. By default, a pipeline's exit status is the status of
its **last** command only. Every earlier stage can fail and the
pipe still reports success, because the last stage succeeded.
Watch a deliberately failing first stage vanish:

```bash
$ cut -d, -f1 missing.csv | sort | head -1
cut: missing.csv: No such file or directory
$ echo $?
0
```

`cut` failed (there is no `missing.csv`), printed its error,
and produced no output, yet the pipeline's exit status is `0`,
success, because `head` ran fine on the empty stream. A script
that checks the exit status here would conclude all is well and
march on with no data.

The fix is the `pipefail` option, which makes a pipeline fail
if **any** stage fails:

```bash
$ set -o pipefail
$ cut -d, -f1 missing.csv | sort | head -1
cut: missing.csv: No such file or directory
$ echo $?
1
```

Same pipe, same error, but now the status is `1` and a script
will notice.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
A pipeline that "worked" because its last stage succeeded is
one of the most common silent failures in shell scripts, and it
is invisible until a missing file or a typo upstream feeds an
empty stream into a step that is perfectly happy to process
nothing. This is exactly why the next chapter's scripts open
with `set -euo pipefail`: the `pipefail` shown here, plus `-e`
to stop on the first failure and `-u` to catch unset variables.
Turn it on for any pipe whose result you act on. At the
interactive prompt it is optional; in a script that decides what
to do next, it is not.
:::

## Quoting: the space that breaks everything

Before a command runs, the shell rewrites the line: it expands
variables, splits the result into separate words on whitespace,
and expands globs. That word-splitting step is the one that
bites. A variable holding a filename with a space in it is, to
the unquoted shell, two words, and the command sees two
arguments where you meant one. This is not an edge case;
spreadsheet exports, downloaded datasets, and "Final Report
v2.csv" all carry spaces.

Make a file with a space in its name and try to count its lines
through an unquoted variable:

```bash
$ f='fy 2011.csv'
$ wc -l $f
0 total
wc: fy: No such file or directory
wc: 2011.csv: No such file or directory
```

The shell split `$f` into `fy` and `2011.csv`, so `wc` looked
for two files that do not exist. Quote the variable and it stays
a single argument:

```bash
$ wc -l "$f"
181 fy 2011.csv
```

The same splitting governs loops (the `for` loop is taught in
Chapter 11's scripting primer; here it is only the vehicle for
showing word-splitting), which is where it quietly corrupts
batch jobs. Unquoted, the loop iterates over the *words*;
quoted, over the *value*:

```bash
$ for w in $f; do echo "  arg: $w"; done
  arg: fy
  arg: 2011.csv

$ for w in "$f"; do echo "  arg: $w"; done
  arg: fy 2011.csv
```

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The rule is mechanical and absolute: **always quote your
variable expansions**, `"$f"`, and prefer the brace form
`"${f}"` when the name butts up against other text
(`"${f}_backup"`). The cost of quoting when you did not need to
is zero; the cost of not quoting when you did is a command run
on the wrong arguments, and next to `rm` that is a deleted file.
The one expansion beginners reach for and mis-remember is the
list of positional arguments: write it `"$@"`, quoted, which
forwards each argument exactly as received; the unquoted `$@`
and the `"$*"` form both mangle arguments that contain spaces
and are almost never what you want. Deliberate word-splitting
(feeding a command a list you built on purpose) exists, but
until you know exactly why you want it, quote everything. A
linter enforces this for you: ShellCheck (Chapter 11) flags
every unquoted expansion, which is reason enough to run it on
every script.
:::

## Globs are not regular expressions

The shell expands a handful of wildcard characters into matching
filenames *before* the command runs. This is **globbing**, and
it is not the same language as the regular expressions that
`grep` and `sed` use (Chapter 8 and Chapter 9 draw that line
carefully). A glob's `*` matches any run of characters in a
filename, `?` matches exactly one, and `[...]` matches one
character from a set. Listing the CSV files in a directory is
the everyday case:

```bash
$ ls *.csv
fy 2011.csv
```

The trap is what happens when a glob matches **nothing**. In
bash, an unmatched glob is not an error and does not expand to
empty; it is passed through to the command **literally**, as the
text you typed:

```bash
$ ls *.xyz
ls: cannot access '*.xyz': No such file or directory
$ echo $?
2
```

`ls` received the literal string `*.xyz` and complained about a
file by that name. This is mostly harmless with `ls`, but put
the same unmatched glob next to a destructive command and the
literal pattern becomes an argument you never intended to pass.
You can make bash expand an unmatched glob to nothing instead,
with the `nullglob` option:

```bash
$ shopt -s nullglob
$ echo "matches:[" *.xyz "]"
matches:[ ]
```

`shopt` is bash's switch for its own optional behaviors [-@gnubash], and
`-s` **s**ets (turns on) the one you name, here `nullglob`;
`shopt -u` unsets it again. With `nullglob` on, the unmatched
`*.xyz` expanded to nothing at all, so `echo` printed the
empty brackets instead of the literal pattern.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
This is one of the sharpest macOS-versus-Linux differences in
the book, because it is a difference in the *default* shell, not
a tool flag. The interactive default on macOS is **zsh**, and
zsh's default for an unmatched glob is the opposite of bash's:
zsh treats it as a hard error, aborts the command, and runs
nothing (its message is "no matches found"). bash passes the
literal pattern through, as shown above. So the very same line
behaves differently depending on whose prompt you are sitting
at, which is why a script should never *rely* on either default;
quote a pattern you mean literally, and check that a glob
matched before feeding it to anything that deletes. The behavior
on both shells, zsh 5.9 (`zsh: no matches found`) and the system
bash 3.2.57 (literal pass-through), is captured in
`transcripts/ch07-divergence-mac.txt`.
:::

## Null-safe file lists: `find -print0` and `xargs -0`

The quoting rule protects a variable you control. A harder case
is a *list* of filenames generated by another command, where you
do not get to quote each one by hand. The usual idiom pipes
`find` into `xargs`: `find . -name '*.csv'` walks the tree from
the current directory (`.`) and prints every name matching the
glob, and `xargs` reads those names from its stdin and appends
them as arguments to a command (`wc -l` here), turning a list
of names into one command line. It splits that list on
whitespace by default, though, and breaks on exactly the
spaced names from two sections ago:

```bash
$ find . -name '*.csv' | xargs wc -l
0 total
wc: ./fy: No such file or directory
wc: 2012.csv: No such file or directory
wc: ./fy: No such file or directory
wc: 2011.csv: No such file or directory
```

`xargs` saw the space inside `fy 2011.csv` as a separator and
handed `wc` broken fragments. The fix is to separate filenames
by a character that cannot appear in a filename: the null byte.
`find -print0` ends each name with a null instead of a newline,
and `xargs -0` reads null-separated input:

```bash
$ find . -name '*.csv' -print0 | xargs -0 wc -l
  181 ./fy 2012.csv
  181 ./fy 2011.csv
  362 total
```

Every name arrives intact, spaces and all. The null byte is the
only safe separator because it is the one character a Unix path
can never contain; even a newline can appear in a filename, so
"split on newlines" (`IFS=$'\n'`) is safer than the default but
still not safe. Reach for the `-print0` / `-0` pair, or `find
-exec ... {} +`, whenever a file list is built by a command
rather than typed by you.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
`find -print0` and `xargs -0` are present on both GNU (Linux)
and BSD (macOS) systems and behave the same way, so the
null-safe idiom is portable; the macOS run in
`transcripts/ch07-divergence-mac.txt` (BSD findutils) produces
the same intact, spaced names. The warning is about the
*unsafe* version: a naive `find | xargs` breaks identically on
both platforms (BSD `wc` even reports `open: No such file`), so
this is not a divergence you can dodge by switching machines. Write the
null-safe form everywhere. Chapter 9 returns to `find` and `fd`
for searching; this is the safety half of the same tool.
:::

## `rm -rf` and the shell with no undo

Now the command this chapter has been circling. `rm` removes
files. `rm -r` removes directories and everything inside them,
recursively. `rm -f` forces, suppressing prompts and ignoring
"no such file." Together, `rm -rf <path>` deletes a whole tree,
silently and immediately, with no trip to a trash folder and no
reflog to undo it. On the running example it is the difference
between deleting a regenerable `output/` directory and deleting
the one copy of a raw dataset you were licensed not to commit.

The danger is rarely the command you type on purpose. It is the
path that is not what you think it is. The worst version is an
**empty variable**: a script builds a path in a variable,
something goes wrong and the variable is empty, and `rm -rf
"$BUILD"/` becomes `rm -rf /`. The defense is to make the shell
refuse an empty value, with the `${VAR:?message}` expansion,
which aborts the command (printing the message) if `VAR` is
unset or empty:

```bash
$ BUILD=''
$ ( rm -rf "${BUILD:?set BUILD first}"/ )
bash: BUILD: set BUILD first
$ echo $?
1
```

The `rm` never ran. With a real value, the same line removes
only the intended subtree and nothing else:

```bash
$ BUILD='project/output'
$ rm -rf "${BUILD:?set BUILD first}"
$ echo $?
0
$ find project
project
project/data
project/data/raw.csv
```

`project/output` is gone; `project/data` is untouched. The habit
that prevents the disaster is duller than any option: look
before you delete. Run `ls` on the exact pattern first, read
what it lists, and only then change `ls` to `rm`. The two
commands take the same arguments, so the `ls` you just verified
is the `rm` you are about to run.

::: {.callout-caution .danger title="DANGER"}
`rm -rf` is the single most destructive command in routine use.
The catalog of ways it goes wrong is short and worth memorizing:
an empty variable (`rm -rf "$X"/` with `X` unset expands to `rm
-rf /`); a stray space (`rm -rf / data` instead of `rm -rf
/data` makes `/` its own argument); a glob that matched more
than you expected (`rm -rf /*`); and running it in the wrong
directory because a `cd` silently failed. Modern GNU `rm`
refuses a bare `rm -rf /` unless you add `--no-preserve-root`,
and BSD (macOS) `rm` errors on `/` as well, so literal
root-deletion is partly guarded now. Never rely on that:
preserve-root does nothing for `rm -rf /*` (the glob expands to
each top-level name, none of which is literally `/`) and nothing
for the far more common disaster, deleting the wrong *non-root*
directory: your data, your home, this project. Defenses, in
order of strength: never put `rm -rf` next to an unguarded
variable; quote every path; guard with `${VAR:?}`; prefer
removing a *named* subdirectory over a pattern; and on your own
machine consider a trash-style tool (`trash`, `rip`) for
interactive deletes so there is a way back. [Appendix A](#sec-panic)
collects the recovery options for when one of these gets past you.
:::

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
If you have just run a destructive `rm` and caught it: **stop
typing**. The deleted data is not in a trash folder, but on a
disk the blocks are often still there until something overwrites
them, so the worst thing you can do is keep working in that
directory. The honest recovery, though, is the one you set up
*beforehand*: a recent commit (for files Git was tracking), a
backup (Time Machine, `rsync` snapshots, Chapter 14), or the
seed-based regeneration this book's example relies on, where
`make` rebuilds a deleted `output/` from code. For an untracked,
unbacked-up raw file, there may be no recovery at all, which is
the entire argument for treating `rm` with the caution this
chapter does.
:::

## Never pipe a stranger into your shell (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

Installation instructions on the open web love this one-liner:

```text
# DANGER: shown to be recognized, never run this as-is
curl -fsSL https://example.com/install.sh | sh
```

It downloads a script and pipes it straight into `sh`, which
runs it immediately, as you, with your permissions, before you
have read a single line of it. This book runs every other
command it shows; it will not run this one, because the whole
point is that you cannot see what you are about to execute.

::: {.callout-caution .danger title="DANGER"}
`curl ... | sh` executes code you have not read, fetched over a
network you do not control. Three concrete risks, current as of
2026: the server can detect that the bytes are being piped
straight into a shell rather than saved to disk, because a
shell consumes the stream line by line and pauses to run each
line (a timing side channel demonstrated publicly since 2016),
and so can serve harmless code to anyone who downloads the file
but malicious code to anyone piping it; there is no checksum or
signature step, so a tampered or man-in-the-middled download
runs just the same; and the script runs with your full user
privileges, free to modify your shell startup files, your SSH
keys, or your `PATH`. A related trap is **PATH-hijacking**: if a
directory you control sits early on your `PATH` and contains a
malicious `python` or `make`, a later command silently runs the
wrong binary. `type -a <name>` shows every match in `PATH`
order, so you can see which one wins:

```bash
$ type -a sort
sort is /usr/bin/sort
sort is /bin/sort
```

Verify before you trust: `command -v <name>` confirms what will
actually run.
:::

The safe pattern is to separate downloading from executing, so
there is a moment in between where you can look. Fetch the
script to a file, read it, verify it against a checksum the
project publishes over a *different* channel, and only then run
it:

```text
# illustrative template; example.com is a placeholder, not run
curl -fsSL -o install.sh https://example.com/install.sh
less install.sh            # actually read it
sha256sum install.sh       # compare to the published hash
sh install.sh              # only after the first three
```

The checksum step uses the same `sha256sum` the pipeline uses to
lock its data hash in Chapter 12; a hash is only as trustworthy
as the channel that delivered the expected value, so take the
expected hash from the project's documentation, not from the
same page that served the script. This is the supply-chain
thread the book returns to in the setup chapter (Chapter 3,
verifying installers) and the AI chapter (Chapter 17, where an
agent that can run shell commands can run exactly this kind of
line on your behalf).

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

These use the asset-pricing project from Chapter 6. Do the
destructive ones in a copy, or in `/tmp`, never in your real
project.

1. Rebuild the distinct-firm count pipe one stage at a time:
   run `cut -d, -f1 data/raw/firm_panel.csv` alone, then add
   `| tail -n +2`, then `| sort -u`, then `| wc -l`, looking at
   the output after each addition. Explain why dropping the
   header changes the final count by exactly one.

2. Run the cleaning step and split its streams: send its results
   to `clean.log` and its diagnostics to `clean.err` in one
   command. Confirm with `cat` that each file holds only its own
   stream. Then redo it merging both into one log, and check the
   redirection order.

3. Turn on `set -o noclobber` and try to overwrite an existing
   file with `>`. Read the refusal, then overwrite it on purpose
   with `>|`. Add `set -o noclobber` to your shell startup file
   if you want it by default (Chapter 18).

4. Make a copy of a data file with a space in its name (`cp
   factors.csv 'fy 2011.csv'`). Count its lines through an
   unquoted variable and watch it break, then fix it with
   quotes. Build a `find ... -print0 | xargs -0 wc -l` that
   counts every CSV safely, spaced names included.

5. Write `rm -rf "${OUT:?set OUT first}"` with `OUT` empty and
   confirm it refuses. Set `OUT` to a throwaway subdirectory and
   confirm it removes only that. Articulate, in one sentence, why
   the `${OUT:?}` guard is worth the extra characters every time.

6. Find a real "curl pipe sh" install line in the wild (many
   popular tools publish one). Without running it, fetch the
   script with `curl -o`, read it with `less`, and locate where
   it writes to your `PATH` or startup files. Decide whether you
   would have run it blind.
